title: Hatchet
excerpt: A guide to preventing Postgres from toppling over.
content_html:
Over the past half year or so, I’ve been writing an internal doc for our engineers trying to distill two years of Postgres battles into a somewhat cohesive document. While I love the Postgres manual, I find it’s hard to turn to when shit hits the fan because it’s just so darn comprehensive. I thought this might be useful for others and would appreciate feedback (or other tidbits that you’ve learned running Postgres in production).

Before starting Hatchet, while I was familiar with SQL, the extent of my knowledge was basically: if a query is slow, you need an index. That’s the starting point for this doc; I’m going to assume you’re familiar with SQL basics, rows, tables, and know roughly what an index is.

And if Claude is writing all of your queries, this might be a waste of time! I recommend supabase/agent-skills

A quick note on ORMs

This guide should still be useful, but you might need to translate some of these tips into your ORM of choice. Lots of optimizations as you scale just aren’t possible with ORMs unless you can break past the abstraction layer and write SQL. You can do this gracefully or non-gracefully; Prisma TypedSQL or equivalents look interesting for this. We use sqlc at Hatchet which gets us very similar behavior; highly recommend if you’re a Go stack.

Table of contents

  • The simple stuff: good reads, writes and schemas
    • Writing a good schema
    • Writing good read queries
    • Writing performant joins
    • Compound indexes and aligning ORDER BY to your indexes
    • Writing good write queries
    • Migrations
    • Connection management
  • Intermediate: the query planner, bulk updates, and autovacuum
    • Introducing the leakiest of abstractions, the query planner
    • Sometimes it just makes sense to seq scan
    • Writing lots of data
    • Default autovacuum settings can kill your database
    • Other types of bloat
  • Some advanced stuff
    • FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED
    • Partitioning
    • Tricks for large table migrations

The simple stuff: good reads, writes and schemas

Let’s start with the basics: queries and schemas at low volume.

Writing a good schema

After you’re deployed, schemas are by far the hardest to change moving forward, so it’s worth spending some time on them. I’d recommend building your schema iteratively: start with a rough approximation for your tables and primary keys, then write some queries on those tables based on your application needs. You can approximate this with some questions: Is this a high-read and/or high-write table? What are the most common filters on reads? Which columns am I updating the most?

If you want to be more formal about it, you can look into database normalization into 1NF/2NF/3NF, but I’ve found normal forms to sometimes be at odds with query efficiency and ease of use, which is critical when you’re moving fast—sometimes it’s just easier to dump data into a jsonb column.

My rules of thumb for schemas are:

  • Use identity columns (auto-incrementing integers, slightly more performant than bigserial) or built-in UUIDs for primary keys
  • Always use timestamptz
  • Always use primary keys
  • Use foreign keys with cascading deletes for low-volume tables, particularly where database consistency and correctness are important. Careful at higher volume.

Writing good read queries

Let’s start with SELECT queries. A useful—albeit slightly inaccurate—mental model for fast selects is: under the hood, Postgres is either going to find a single row in a table very quickly, or it’s going to read every single row in your table using something called a sequential scan 😞.

It’s going to find a single row very quickly when you filter by:

  • An explicit index
  • A unique constraint (just a special case of index)
  • A primary key (these are automatically indexed in Postgres)

Indexes by default use a btree implementation. It’s most helpful to think of indexes as just another table in Postgres, with data stored in a specific format which is optimized for lookups (more on this later). These trees are great because finding a single row happens in approximately log(n) time, where n is the number of rows in the table—in other words, really fast.

When Postgres can’t use an index, it’ll use something called a sequential scan, or seq scan. Seq scans are much slower than index lookups, but modern databases are so fast at loading rows into memory that you probably won’t even notice at first: seq scans on tables with less than 20k rows are pretty much instant.

Writing performant joins

For inner joins, there’s rarely an argument for not using primary keys as the inner join; it usually speaks to a schema design or normalization problem. Treat ON clauses with the same respect as a WHERE clause—the same principles apply. Use an index.

Compound indexes and aligning ORDER BY to your indexes

Often the first slow query in your application will be a list query across a large table. Something like:

Loading syntax highlighting...

In this case, you can use a compound index—a sensible one might be:

Loading syntax highlighting...

In more complex cases, a good rule of thumb is: the ORDER BY columns should be the last columns in the index, and you should align columns to the ordering in the ORDER BY. Note that Postgres can scan btrees in both directions, so sometimes the DESC is irrelevant—but for compound indexes it’s good practice. More information here.

Writing good write queries

The premise of successful writes is:

  1. Keep transactions short. Don’t go querying an external service in the middle of a transaction unless you have a really good reason to.
  2. Be careful of the rows you’re locking for writing; in other words, only lock what you need. Every time you update a row, you’re taking out a lock on that row for a short period of time until the transaction commits.

As your system gets busier, you’re going to start noticing the impact of locks more. In particular, you might try to create an index at some point in the future with a simple CREATE INDEX command: turns out this locks your table and prevents inserts and updates! When creating an index on an existing large table, always use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

Migrations

Getting really good at writing migrations is an important technical advantage: it helps you iterate much faster and increases your uptime. As a starting point, try to keep migrations additive (in other words, don’t delete or remove columns) and run them in a transaction wherever possible; this will make rollbacks and partial migrations much easier to deal with. As you get more advanced, you can start looking into expand and contract migrations.

The simplest mental model for good migrations is: does this block all of my writes, or does it not? Creating an index without CONCURRENTLY blocks all your writes, so you might see downtime. Generally, operations which call ALTER TABLE should be worth a second look; for example, adding a new check constraint to a very large table can block your writes as well (unless you add it with the NOT VALID keyword).

Connection management

Every time you execute a transaction or query against your database, you’re utilizing a connection. Connections are expensive in a number of dimensions (cpu and memory), and high connection churn can lead to a lot of unnecessary resource waste, so connections should be long-lived. Connection storms (when you start using up a ton of new connections at the same time) can also lead to very hard to debug edge cases related to internal Postgres locks.

Because of all these connection footguns, external connection poolers like pgbouncer are great! If you can’t add this for whatever reason, in-memory connection poolers are a great second option. For example, because Hatchet is open-source, we don’t assume that all user databases use connection poolers, so we use pgxpool (an in-memory connection pool for Go) for this purpose.

Intermediate: the query planner, bulk updates, and autovacuum

Introducing the leakiest of abstractions, the query planner

At a certain point, your queries might become complex enough that a simple index won’t cut it (and you shouldn’t endlessly add indexes to your tables—they come with overhead). The queries might involve many JOIN statements or different types of joins where the correct path for querying the data isn’t clear.

At this point, you will need to concern yourself with the query planner. At best, the query planner is a leaky abstraction. It’s an internal implementation, and you have virtually no control over it, but you have to know its spontaneous and sometimes irrational behavior. It’s like working with an LLM!

The query planner looks at the query you pass in, and it figures out how it should translate your query into a set of internal operations in the database. For example, it might look at your query, and realize that it needs to use an index. In an ideal world, the query planner would know, for every query and set of parameters, the perfect plan to use. But the query planner is operating on limited information, and sometimes it doesn’t pick the best option.

This limited information is the table statistics. You can actually query it directly in Postgres:

Loading syntax highlighting...

These statistics are collected for every ANALYZE. This also happens when autovacuum is run (see below), so more frequent autovacuums also mean that your query statistics will be more up to date. A common reason why your query is behaving improperly is not analyzing frequently enough.

The reason I think it’s useful to view queries as binary—they either seq scan or they don’t seq scan—is: the more you micro-optimize a query, the more of a risk you take that the query planner goes rogue. If you stick to querying by primary keys and indexes, the query planner will have a much easier time.

Let’s say that there’s nothing obviously wrong in your query, but it’s still slow—how do you go about debugging this? Some Postgres database providers (like Google CloudSQL) will sample your queries and save slow ones—but many don’t. This is where EXPLAIN ANALYZE is your friend. This outputs the query plan for the query and executes the query (careful running this in production—you can use EXPLAIN without ANALYZE to get a query plan), and then compares its estimates based on the table statistics to the actual number of rows scanned. I usually place my sql query in a file, prefix it with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, COSTS, VERBOSE, BUFFERS, FORMAT JSON) and run:

Loading syntax highlighting...

And then use explain.dalibo.com to visualize the execution plan.

Sometimes it just makes sense to seq scan

There are cases where you think an index should be used, but the query planner is still seq scanning anyway, despite table statistics being up to date and the index being valid. In these cases, Postgres is usually estimating that the cost of the seq scan will be smaller than the cost of the index scan. Index scans do come with some overhead; indexes are stored separately from the actual data in the table (called the heap)—finding all of the rows in the heap can be expensive!

Unless you can dramatically restructure your query, you might have to accept that it’s going to seq scan, or think about something like partitioning (more on that below).

Writing lots of data

Let’s say your application is scaling and you need to write a lot of data fast. Each query has some overhead associated with it (separate from the connection overhead we talked about before): this includes the round-trip time to the database, the time it takes the internal application connection pool to acquire a connection, and the time it takes Postgres to process the query (including a set of internal Postgres locks which can be bottlenecks in high-throughput scenarios).

To reduce this overhead, we can pack a batch of rows into each query. The simplest way to do this is to send all queries to the Postgres server at once in an implicit transaction (in Go, we can use pgx to execute a SendBatch). Batching is very powerful: we found that it can ~10× your throughput. I wrote more about this plus some other tips for writing data quickly here.

Default autovacuum settings can kill your database

Autovacuum is a critical operation in Postgres databases that sometimes needs to be tuned, especially in high-write scenarios. The autovacuum daemon is responsible for a number of things, including cleaning up dead tuples and managing transaction ids.

What’s a dead tuple? A tuple is an instance of a row on the filesystem. Every time you update or delete a row, a version of that row is left in Postgres until all transactions which started before that row was updated or deleted have committed or rolled back. These rows which can no longer be read by any transactions are dead tuples.

If you’re writing data quickly enough, sometimes autovacuum can’t keep up, which will get you into a very unhealthy state, very quickly. You’ll see this when you query for active processes on the database:

Loading syntax highlighting...

If you see an autovacuum query running for more than ~1 hour, you might want to consider changing your autovacuum settings! See this article for more information.

It’s worth monitoring this: if you use up all transaction ids in the system before they can be reclaimed by autovacuum, you’ll reach a dreaded state called transaction id wraparound. This will mean a big chunk of downtime.

Other types of bloat

Besides dead tuples, there are two other kinds of bloat you’ll often encounter in a busy Postgres system:

  1. Table bloat caused by partially filled pages. Postgres stores rows on pages on disk, each of which are 8kb in size. When Postgres can’t fit new rows onto an existing page, it creates a new one. But when dead tuples are reclaimed, this can lead to pages not being entirely filled, which can increase the disk usage of Postgres, sometimes significantly. The best way to avoid table bloat is by tuning autovacuum before you’re bloated. But there are some extensions to help with bloated tables, like pg_repack, because the built-in Postgres VACUUM FULL is rarely a good idea. Note that Postgres 19 is getting REPACK...CONCURRENTLY, which I haven’t tested, but seems like potentially a good solution for concurrent table repacking.
  2. Index bloat is a special case of table bloat, and is similarly solved by good autovacuum settings. But Postgres has a built-in command for dealing with this, which is REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

Some advanced stuff

I wanted to end with a set of advanced Postgres features which have been particularly useful for us at Hatchet.

FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED

The best way to think about this Postgres feature is that it reserves the rows that you’re selecting for use in your transaction without interfering with other queries. We use it primarily for implementing our job queue; a single-query queue in Postgres can be implemented like this:

Loading syntax highlighting...

It’s also very useful in cases where you’re doing many independent updates of rows, or you’re managing leases on objects in your system across many instances of your application (for example, we use this to distribute tenant leases across Hatchet engines).

Partitioning

Postgres has built-in partitioning which allows you to subdivide your tables based on row values, like timestamps or hashes. This can be incredibly useful for time-series data (in our case, historical task data) because:

  1. Each partition can be autovacuumed independently, allowing you to scale up autovacs on your table
  2. Deleting old data is near-instant—you simply drop the table partition, rather than iterating through rows

Partitioning does come with drawbacks: there can be overhead on read queries if Postgres doesn’t prune partitions during the planning phase (something that Postgres has gotten much better at in recent releases).

I’ve written more about our experience with partitioning here.

Tricks for large table migrations

(note that this isn’t referring to a database migration, but rather moving large amounts of data from one table to another, which we find ourselves doing a few times a year)

If you try to migrate really large tables, it can take many hours to copy data in a single transaction. This isn’t good—long-running transactions prevent autovacuum from doing its job properly, and therefore make the entire system bloated with dead tuples. Also if you want to keep writing to the old tables, the new table won’t see the new data.

So we need to figure out how to migrate data safely without a transaction, with new writes (after the migration has started) being copied to the new table. One of the tricks we’ve learned is to use Postgres triggers and run a large batched backfill outside of a transaction, using unique constraints on the primary key to prevent duplicate writes.

That’s it! If you have any other tidbits about Postgres scaling—or questions about Postgres scaling in general—feel free to reach out.

It’s also very useful in cases where you’re doing many independent updates of rows, or you’re managing leases on objects in your system across many instances of your application (for example, we use this to distribute tenant leases across Hatchet engines). Partitioning Postgres has built-in partitioning which allows you to subdivide your tables based on row values, like timestamps or hashes. This can be incredibly useful for time-series data (in our case, historical task data) because: Each partition can be autovacuumed independently, allowing you to scale up autovacs on your table Deleting old data is near-instant—you simply drop the table partition, rather than iterating through rows Partitioning does come with drawbacks: there can be overhead on read queries if Postgres doesn’t prune partitions during the planning phase (something that Postgres has gotten much better at in recent releases). I’ve written more about our experience with partitioning here. Tricks for large table migrations (note that this isn’t referring to a database migration, but rather moving large amounts of data from one table to another, which we find ourselves doing a few times a year) If you try to migrate really large tables, it can take many hours to copy data in a single transaction. This isn’t good—long-running transactions prevent autovacuum from doing its job properly, and therefore make the entire system bloated with dead tuples. Also if you want to keep writing to the old tables, the new table won’t see the new data. So we need to figure out how to migrate data safely without a transaction, with new writes (after the migration has started) being copied to the new table. One of the tricks we’ve learned is to use Postgres triggers and run a large batched backfill outside of a transaction, using unique constraints on the primary key to prevent duplicate writes. That’s it! If you have any other tidbits about Postgres scaling—or questions about Postgres scaling in general—feel free to reach out.

blocks.html_en: After you’re deployed, schemas are by far the hardest to change moving forward, so it’s worth spending some time on them. I’d recommend building your schema iteratively: start with a rough approximation for your tables and primary keys, then write some queries on those tables based on your application needs. You can approximate this with some questions: Is this a high-read and/or high-write table? What are the most common filters on reads? Which columns am I updating the most?

sentences.en: After you’re deployed, schemas are by far the hardest to change moving forward, so it’s worth spending some time on them.
sentences.zh: 部署后，模式是迄今为止最难更改的，因此值得花一些时间在它们上。

sentences.en: I’d recommend building your schema iteratively: start with a rough approximation for your tables and primary keys, then write some queries on those tables based on your application needs.
sentences.zh: 我建议迭代地构建您的架构：从表和主键的粗略近似开始，然后根据您的应用程序需求在这些表上编写一些查询。

sentences.en: Which columns am I updating the most?

blocks.html_en: • Use identity columns (auto-incrementing integers, slightly more performant than bigserial) or built-in UUIDs for primary keys

blocks.html_en: • Use foreign keys with cascading deletes for low-volume tables, particularly where database consistency and correctness are important. Careful at higher volume.

sentences.zh: 对于小容量表，使用带有级联删除的外键，特别是在数据库一致性和正确性很重要的情况下。

blocks.html_en: Let’s start with SELECT queries. A useful—albeit slightly inaccurate—mental model for fast selects is: under the hood, Postgres is either going to find a single row in a table very quickly, or it’s going to read every single row in your table using something called a sequential scan 😞.

sentences.en: Let’s start with SELECT queries.
sentences.zh: 让我们从 SELECT 查询开始。

sentences.en: A useful—albeit slightly inaccurate—mental model for fast selects is: under the hood, Postgres is either going to find a single row in a table very quickly, or it’s going to read every single row in your table using something called a sequential scan 😞.
sentences.zh: 一个有用的（尽管有点不准确）快速选择的心理模型是：在幕后，Postgres 要么会非常快速地找到表中的一行，要么会使用称为顺序扫描的方法读取表中的每一行。

blocks.html_en: It’s going to find a single row very quickly when you filter by:

blocks.html_en: • A primary key (these are automatically indexed in Postgres)

sentences.zh: 主键（这些在 Postgres 中自动索引）

blocks.html_en: Indexes by default use a btree implementation. It’s most helpful to think of indexes as just another table in Postgres, with data stored in a specific format which is optimized for lookups (more on this later). These trees are great because finding a single row happens in approximately log(n) time, where n is the number of rows in the table—in other words, really fast.

sentences.en: Indexes by default use a btree implementation.
sentences.zh: 索引默认使用 btree 实现。

sentences.en: It’s most helpful to think of indexes as just another table in Postgres, with data stored in a specific format which is optimized for lookups (more on this later).
sentences.zh: 将索引视为 Postgres 中的另一个表是最有帮助的，其中数据以特定格式存储，该格式针对查找进行了优化（稍后会详细介绍）。

sentences.en: These trees are great because finding a single row happens in approximately log(n) time, where n is the number of rows in the table—in other words, really fast.
sentences.zh: 这些树很棒，因为找到一行大约需要 log(n) 时间，其中 n 是表中的行数，换句话说，速度非常快。

blocks.html_en: When Postgres can’t use an index, it’ll use something called a sequential scan, or seq scan. Seq scans are much slower than index lookups, but modern databases are so fast at loading rows into memory that you probably won’t even notice at first: seq scans on tables with less than 20k rows are pretty much instant.

sentences.en: When Postgres can’t use an index, it’ll use something called a sequential scan, or seq scan.
sentences.zh: 当 Postgres 无法使用索引时，它将使用称为顺序扫描或 seq 扫描的方法。

sentences.en: Seq scans are much slower than index lookups, but modern databases are so fast at loading rows into memory that you probably won’t even notice at first: seq scans on tables with less than 20k rows are pretty much instant.
sentences.zh: Seq 扫描比索引查找慢得多，但现代数据库将行加载到内存中的速度非常快，您可能一开始都没有注意到：对少于 20k 行的表进行 seq 扫描几乎是即时的。

Note that Postgres can scan btrees in both directions, so sometimes the DESC is irrelevant—but for compound indexes it’s good practice. More information here.

blocks.html_en: Writing good write queries

sentences.en: Writing good write queries
sentences.zh: 编写良好的写入查询

blocks.html_en: The premise of successful writes is:

sentences.en: The premise of successful writes is:
sentences.zh: 写入成功的前提是：

blocks.html_en: • Keep transactions short. Don’t go querying an external service in the middle of a transaction unless you have a really good reason to.

sentences.en: Keep transactions short.
sentences.zh: 保持交易简短。

sentences.en: Don’t go querying an external service in the middle of a transaction unless you have a really good reason to.
sentences.zh: 除非有充分的理由，否则不要在事务中间查询外部服务。

blocks.html_en: • Be careful of the rows you’re locking for writing; in other words, only lock what you need. Every time you update a row, you’re taking out a lock on that row for a short period of time until the transaction commits.

sentences.en: Be careful of the rows you’re locking for writing; in other words, only lock what you need.
sentences.zh: 小心您锁定写入的行；换句话说，只锁定您需要的内容。

sentences.en: Every time you update a row, you’re taking out a lock on that row for a short period of time until the transaction commits.
sentences.zh: 每次更新一行时，都会在短时间内对该行进行锁定，直到事务提交。

blocks.html_en: As your system gets busier, you’re going to start noticing the impact of locks more. In particular, you might try to create an index at some point in the future with a simple CREATE INDEX command: turns out this locks your table and prevents inserts and updates! When creating an index on an existing large table, always use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

sentences.en: As your system gets busier, you’re going to start noticing the impact of locks more.
sentences.zh: 随着您的系统变得更加繁忙，您将开始更多地注意到锁的影响。

sentences.en: In particular, you might try to create an index at some point in the future with a simple CREATE INDEX command: turns out this locks your table and prevents inserts and updates!
sentences.zh: 特别是，您可能会尝试在将来的某个时候使用简单的 CREATE INDEX 命令创建索引：结果这会锁定您的表并阻止插入和更新！

sentences.en: When creating an index on an existing large table, always use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.
sentences.zh: 在现有大型表上创建索引时，请始终使用 CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY。

blocks.html_en: Migrations

sentences.en: Migrations
sentences.zh: 迁移

blocks.html_en: Getting really good at writing migrations is an important technical advantage: it helps you iterate much faster and increases your uptime. As a starting point, try to keep migrations additive (in other words, don’t delete or remove columns) and run them in a transaction wherever possible; this will make rollbacks and partial migrations much easier to deal with. As you get more advanced, you can start looking into expand and contract migrations.

sentences.en: Getting really good at writing migrations is an important technical advantage: it helps you iterate much faster and increases your uptime.
sentences.zh: 真正擅长编写迁移是一项重要的技术优势：它可以帮助您更快地迭代并增加正常运行时间。

sentences.en: As a starting point, try to keep migrations additive (in other words, don’t delete or remove columns) and run them in a transaction wherever possible; this will make rollbacks and partial migrations much easier to deal with.
sentences.zh: 作为起点，尝试保持迁移附加（换句话说，不要删除或删除列）并尽可能在事务中运行它们；这将使回滚和部分迁移更容易处理。

sentences.en: As you get more advanced, you can start looking into expand and contract migrations.
sentences.zh: 当您变得更高级时，您可以开始研究扩展和收缩迁移。

blocks.html_en: The simplest mental model for good migrations is: does this block all of my writes, or does it not? Creating an index without CONCURRENTLY blocks all your writes, so you might see downtime. Generally, operations which call ALTER TABLE should be worth a second look; for example, adding a new check constraint to a very large table can block your writes as well (unless you add it with the NOT VALID keyword).

sentences.en: The simplest mental model for good migrations is: does this block all of my writes, or does it not?
sentences.zh: 良好迁移的最简单心理模型是：这是否会阻止我所有的写入？

sentences.en: Creating an index without CONCURRENTLY blocks all your writes, so you might see downtime.
sentences.zh: 创建没有 CONCURRENTLY 的索引会阻止所有写入，因此您可能会遇到停机情况。

sentences.en: Generally, operations which call ALTER TABLE should be worth a second look; for example, adding a new check constraint to a very large table can block your writes as well (unless you add it with the NOT VALID keyword).
sentences.zh: 一般来说，调用 ALTER TABLE 的操作值得再看一下；例如，向非常大的表添加新的检查约束也会阻止您的写入（除非您使用 NOT VALID 关键字添加它）。

blocks.html_en: Connection management

sentences.en: Connection management
sentences.zh: 连接管理

blocks.html_en: Every time you execute a transaction or query against your database, you’re utilizing a connection. Connections are expensive in a number of dimensions (cpu and memory), and high connection churn can lead to a lot of unnecessary resource waste, so connections should be long-lived. Connection storms (when you start using up a ton of new connections at the same time) can also lead to very hard to debug edge cases related to internal Postgres locks.

sentences.en: Every time you execute a transaction or query against your database, you’re utilizing a connection.
sentences.zh: 每次对数据库执行事务或查询时，您都在利用连接。

sentences.en: Connections are expensive in a number of dimensions (cpu and memory), and high connection churn can lead to a lot of unnecessary resource waste, so connections should be long-lived.
sentences.zh: 连接在多个维度（CPU 和内存）都是昂贵的，高连接流失率会导致大量不必要的资源浪费，因此连接应该是长期存在的。

sentences.en: Connection storms (when you start using up a ton of new connections at the same time) can also lead to very hard to debug edge cases related to internal Postgres locks.
sentences.zh: 连接风暴（当您同时开始使用大量新连接时）也可能导致很难调试与内部 Postgres 锁相关的边缘情况。

blocks.html_en: Because of all these connection footguns, external connection poolers like pgbouncer are great! If you can’t add this for whatever reason, in-memory connection poolers are a great second option. For example, because Hatchet is open-source, we don’t assume that all user databases use connection poolers, so we use pgxpool (an in-memory connection pool for Go) for this purpose.

sentences.en: Because of all these connection footguns, external connection poolers like pgbouncer are great!
sentences.zh: 由于所有这些连接枪，像 pgbouncer 这样的外部连接池非常棒！

sentences.en: If you can’t add this for whatever reason, in-memory connection poolers are a great second option.
sentences.zh: 如果您出于某种原因无法添加它，那么内存连接池是第二个不错的选择。

sentences.en: For example, because Hatchet is open-source, we don’t assume that all user databases use connection poolers, so we use pgxpool (an in-memory connection pool for Go) for this purpose.
sentences.zh: 例如，由于 Hatchet 是开源的，我们并不假设所有用户数据库都使用连接池，因此我们使用 pgxpool（Go 的内存连接池）来实现此目的。

blocks.html_en: Intermediate: the query planner, bulk updates, and autovacuum

sentences.en: Intermediate: the query planner, bulk updates, and autovacuum
sentences.zh: 中级：查询规划器、批量更新和 autovacuum

blocks.html_en: Introducing the leakiest of abstractions, the query planner

sentences.en: Introducing the leakiest of abstractions, the query planner
sentences.zh: 引入最泄漏的抽象，查询规划器

blocks.html_en: At a certain point, your queries might become complex enough that a simple index won’t cut it (and you shouldn’t endlessly add indexes to your tables—they come with overhead). The queries might involve many JOIN statements or different types of joins where the correct path for querying the data isn’t clear.

sentences.en: At a certain point, your queries might become complex enough that a simple index won’t cut it (and you shouldn’t endlessly add indexes to your tables—they come with overhead).
sentences.zh: 在某个时刻，您的查询可能会变得足够复杂，以至于简单的索引无法解决问题（并且您不应该无休止地向表中添加索引 - 它们会带来开销）。

sentences.en: The queries might involve many JOIN statements or different types of joins where the correct path for querying the data isn’t clear.
sentences.zh: 查询可能涉及许多 JOIN 语句或不同类型的联接，其中查询数据的正确路径不清楚。

blocks.html_en: At this point, you will need to concern yourself with the query planner. At best, the query planner is a leaky abstraction. It’s an internal implementation, and you have virtually no control over it, but you have to know its spontaneous and sometimes irrational behavior. It’s like working with an LLM!

sentences.en: At this point, you will need to concern yourself with the query planner.
sentences.zh: 此时，您需要关注查询规划器。

sentences.en: At best, the query planner is a leaky abstraction.
sentences.zh: 查询规划器充其量只是一个有漏洞的抽象。

sentences.en: It’s an internal implementation, and you have virtually no control over it, but you have to know its spontaneous and sometimes irrational behavior.
sentences.zh: 这是一个内部实现，你几乎无法控制它，但你必须知道它的自发行为，有时甚至是非理性的行为。

sentences.en: It’s like working with an LLM!
sentences.zh: 这就像与法学硕士一起工作！

blocks.html_en: The query planner looks at the query you pass in, and it figures out how it should translate your query into a set of internal operations in the database. For example, it might look at your query, and realize that it needs to use an index. In an ideal world, the query planner would know, for every query and set of parameters, the perfect plan to use. But the query planner is operating on limited information, and sometimes it doesn’t pick the best option.

sentences.en: The query planner looks at the query you pass in, and it figures out how it should translate your query into a set of internal operations in the database.
sentences.zh: 查询规划器查看您传入的查询，并确定如何将您的查询转换为数据库中的一组内部操作。

sentences.en: For example, it might look at your query, and realize that it needs to use an index.
sentences.zh: 例如，它可能会查看您的查询，并意识到它需要使用索引。

sentences.en: In an ideal world, the query planner would know, for every query and set of parameters, the perfect plan to use.
sentences.zh: 在理想的情况下，查询规划器会知道对于每个查询和参数集，要使用的完美计划。

sentences.en: But the query planner is operating on limited information, and sometimes it doesn’t pick the best option.
sentences.zh: 但查询规划器基于有限的信息进行操作，有时它不会选择最佳选项。

blocks.html_en: This limited information is the table statistics. You can actually query it directly in Postgres:

sentences.en: This limited information is the table statistics.
sentences.zh: 这些有限的信息就是表统计信息。

sentences.en: You can actually query it directly in Postgres:
sentences.zh: 其实你可以直接在Postgres中查询：

blocks.html_en: Loading syntax highlighting...

sentences.en: Loading syntax highlighting...
sentences.zh: 正在加载语法高亮显示...

blocks.html_en: These statistics are collected for every ANALYZE. This also happens when autovacuum is run (see below), so more frequent autovacuums also mean that your query statistics will be more up to date. A common reason why your query is behaving improperly is not analyzing frequently enough.

sentences.en: These statistics are collected for every ANALYZE.
sentences.zh: 这些统计数据是为每次分析收集的。

sentences.en: This also happens when autovacuum is run (see below), so more frequent autovacuums also mean that your query statistics will be more up to date.
sentences.zh: 运行 autovacuum 时也会发生这种情况（见下文），因此更频繁的 autovacuum 也意味着您的查询统计信息将更加最新。

sentences.en: A common reason why your query is behaving improperly is not analyzing frequently enough.
sentences.zh: 查询行为不正确的一个常见原因是分析不够频繁。

blocks.html_en: The reason I think it’s useful to view queries as binary—they either seq scan or they don’t seq scan—is: the more you micro-optimize a query, the more of a risk you take that the query planner goes rogue. If you stick to querying by primary keys and indexes, the query planner will have a much easier time.

sentences.en: The reason I think it’s useful to view queries as binary—they either seq scan or they don’t seq scan—is: the more you micro-optimize a query, the more of a risk you take that the query planner goes rogue.
sentences.zh: 我认为将查询视为二进制（它们要么进行序列扫描，要么不进行序列扫描）很有用的原因是：对查询进行微优化越多，查询规划器出错的风险就越大。

sentences.en: If you stick to querying by primary keys and indexes, the query planner will have a much easier time.
sentences.zh: 如果您坚持通过主键和索引进行查询，查询规划器将会轻松得多。

blocks.html_en: Let’s say that there’s nothing obviously wrong in your query, but it’s still slow—how do you go about debugging this? Some Postgres database providers (like Google CloudSQL) will sample your queries and save slow ones—but many don’t. This is where EXPLAIN ANALYZE is your friend. This outputs the query plan for the query and executes the query (careful running this in production—you can use EXPLAIN without ANALYZE to get a query plan), and then compares its estimates based on the table statistics to the actual number of rows scanned. I usually place my sql query in a file, prefix it with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, COSTS, VERBOSE, BUFFERS, FORMAT JSON) and run:

sentences.en: Let’s say that there’s nothing obviously wrong in your query, but it’s still slow—how do you go about debugging this?
sentences.zh: 假设您的查询中没有任何明显的错误，但它仍然很慢 - 您如何着手调试它？

sentences.en: Some Postgres database providers (like Google CloudSQL) will sample your queries and save slow ones—but many don’t.
sentences.zh: 一些 Postgres 数据库提供商（例如 Google CloudSQL）会对您的查询进行采样并保存速度较慢的查询，但许多提供商不会这样做。

sentences.en: This is where EXPLAIN ANALYZE is your friend.
sentences.zh: 这就是 EXPLAIN ANALYZE 为您提供帮助的地方。

sentences.en: This outputs the query plan for the query and executes the query (careful running this in production—you can use EXPLAIN without ANALYZE to get a query plan), and then compares its estimates based on the table statistics to the actual number of rows scanned.
sentences.zh: 这将输出查询的查询计划并执行查询（在生产中小心运行此查询 - 您可以使用不带 ANALYZE 的 EXPLAIN 来获取查询计划），然后将其基于表统计信息的估计值与扫描的实际行数进行比较。

sentences.en: I usually place my sql query in a file, prefix it with EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, COSTS, VERBOSE, BUFFERS, FORMAT JSON) and run:
sentences.zh: 我通常将 sql 查询放在一个文件中，在其前面添加 EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, COSTS, VERBOSE, BUFFERS, FORMAT JSON) 前缀，然后运行：

blocks.html_en: And then use explain.dalibo.com to visualize the execution plan.

sentences.en: And then use explain.dalibo.com to visualize the execution plan.
sentences.zh: 然后使用explain.dalibo.com可视化执行计划。

blocks.html_en: Sometimes it just makes sense to seq scan

sentences.en: Sometimes it just makes sense to seq scan
sentences.zh: 有时顺序扫描才有意义

blocks.html_en: There are cases where you think an index should be used, but the query planner is still seq scanning anyway, despite table statistics being up to date and the index being valid. In these cases, Postgres is usually estimating that the cost of the seq scan will be smaller than the cost of the index scan. Index scans do come with some overhead; indexes are stored separately from the actual data in the table (called the heap)—finding all of the rows in the heap can be expensive!

sentences.en: There are cases where you think an index should be used, but the query planner is still seq scanning anyway, despite table statistics being up to date and the index being valid.
sentences.zh: 在某些情况下，您认为应该使用索引，但查询计划程序仍然进行 seq 扫描，尽管表统计信息是最新的并且索引是有效的。

sentences.en: In these cases, Postgres is usually estimating that the cost of the seq scan will be smaller than the cost of the index scan.
sentences.zh: 在这些情况下，Postgres 通常估计 seq 扫描的成本将小于索引扫描的成本。

sentences.en: Index scans do come with some overhead; indexes are stored separately from the actual data in the table (called the heap)—finding all of the rows in the heap can be expensive!
sentences.zh: 索引扫描确实会带来一些开销；索引与表中的实际数据（称为堆）分开存储 - 查找堆中的所有行可能会很昂贵！

blocks.html_en: Unless you can dramatically restructure your query, you might have to accept that it’s going to seq scan, or think about something like partitioning (more on that below).

sentences.en: Unless you can dramatically restructure your query, you might have to accept that it’s going to seq scan, or think about something like partitioning (more on that below).
sentences.zh: 除非您可以显着地重构您的查询，否则您可能必须接受它将进行顺序扫描，或者考虑诸如分区之类的东西（更多内容见下文）。

blocks.html_en: Writing lots of data

sentences.en: Writing lots of data
sentences.zh: 写入大量数据

blocks.html_en: Let’s say your application is scaling and you need to write a lot of data fast. Each query has some overhead associated with it (separate from the connection overhead we talked about before): this includes the round-trip time to the database, the time it takes the internal application connection pool to acquire a connection, and the time it takes Postgres to process the query (including a set of internal Postgres locks which can be bottlenecks in high-throughput scenarios).

sentences.en: Let’s say your application is scaling and you need to write a lot of data fast.
sentences.zh: 假设您的应用程序正在扩展，并且您需要快速写入大量数据。

sentences.en: Each query has some overhead associated with it (separate from the connection overhead we talked about before): this includes the round-trip time to the database, the time it takes the internal application connection pool to acquire a connection, and the time it takes Postgres to process the query (including a set of internal Postgres locks which can be bottlenecks in high-throughput scenarios).
sentences.zh: 每个查询都有一些与之相关的开销（与我们之前讨论的连接开销分开）：这包括到数据库的往返时间、内部应用程序连接池获取连接所需的时间以及 Postgres 处理查询所需的时间（包括一组内部 Postgres 锁，这些锁在高吞吐量场景中可能成为瓶颈）。

blocks.html_en: To reduce this overhead, we can pack a batch of rows into each query. The simplest way to do this is to send all queries to the Postgres server at once in an implicit transaction (in Go, we can use pgx to execute a SendBatch). Batching is very powerful: we found that it can ~10× your throughput. I wrote more about this plus some other tips for writing data quickly here.

sentences.en: To reduce this overhead, we can pack a batch of rows into each query.
sentences.zh: 为了减少这种开销，我们可以将一批行打包到每个查询中。

sentences.en: The simplest way to do this is to send all queries to the Postgres server at once in an implicit transaction (in Go, we can use pgx to execute a SendBatch).
sentences.zh: 最简单的方法是在隐式事务中一次性将所有查询发送到 Postgres 服务器（在 Go 中，我们可以使用 pgx 来执行 SendBatch）。

sentences.en: Batching is very powerful: we found that it can ~10× your throughput.
sentences.zh: 批处理非常强大：我们发现它可以将吞吐量提高约 10 倍。

sentences.en: I wrote more about this plus some other tips for writing data quickly here.
sentences.zh: 我在这里写了更多相关内容以及一些快速写入数据的其他技巧。

blocks.html_en: Default autovacuum settings can kill your database

sentences.en: Default autovacuum settings can kill your database
sentences.zh: 默认 autovacuum 设置可能会杀死您的数据库

blocks.html_en: Autovacuum is a critical operation in Postgres databases that sometimes needs to be tuned, especially in high-write scenarios. The autovacuum daemon is responsible for a number of things, including cleaning up dead tuples and managing transaction ids.

sentences.en: Autovacuum is a critical operation in Postgres databases that sometimes needs to be tuned, especially in high-write scenarios.
sentences.zh: Autovacuum 是 Postgres 数据库中的一项关键操作，有时需要进行调整，尤其是在高写入场景下。

sentences.en: The autovacuum daemon is responsible for a number of things, including cleaning up dead tuples and managing transaction ids.
sentences.zh: autovacuum 守护进程负责很多事情，包括清理死元组和管理事务 ID。

blocks.html_en: What’s a dead tuple? A tuple is an instance of a row on the filesystem. Every time you update or delete a row, a version of that row is left in Postgres until all transactions which started before that row was updated or deleted have committed or rolled back. These rows which can no longer be read by any transactions are dead tuples.

sentences.en: What’s a dead tuple?
sentences.zh: 什么是死元组？

sentences.en: A tuple is an instance of a row on the filesystem.
sentences.zh: 元组是文件系统上行的实例。

sentences.en: Every time you update or delete a row, a version of that row is left in Postgres until all transactions which started before that row was updated or deleted have committed or rolled back.
sentences.zh: 每次更新或删除一行时，该行的一个版本都会保留在 Postgres 中，直到更新或删除该行之前启动的所有事务都已提交或回滚。

sentences.en: These rows which can no longer be read by any transactions are dead tuples.
sentences.zh: 这些无法再被任何事务读取的行是死元组。

blocks.html_en: If you’re writing data quickly enough, sometimes autovacuum can’t keep up, which will get you into a very unhealthy state, very quickly. You’ll see this when you query for active processes on the database:

sentences.en: If you’re writing data quickly enough, sometimes autovacuum can’t keep up, which will get you into a very unhealthy state, very quickly.
sentences.zh: 如果你写入数据的速度足够快，有时 autovacuum 无法跟上，这会让你很快进入一种非常不健康的状态。

sentences.en: You’ll see this when you query for active processes on the database:
sentences.zh: 当您在数据库上查询活动进程时，您会看到这一点：

blocks.html_en: Loading syntax highlighting...

sentences.en: Loading syntax highlighting...
sentences.zh: 正在加载语法高亮显示...

blocks.html_en: If you see an autovacuum query running for more than ~1 hour, you might want to consider changing your autovacuum settings! See this article for more information.

sentences.en: If you see an autovacuum query running for more than ~1 hour, you might want to consider changing your autovacuum settings!
sentences.zh: 如果您看到 autovacuum 查询运行超过约 1 小时，您可能需要考虑更改 autovacuum 设置！

sentences.en: See this article for more information.
sentences.zh: 请参阅这篇文章了解更多信息。

blocks.html_en: It’s worth monitoring this: if you use up all transaction ids in the system before they can be reclaimed by autovacuum, you’ll reach a dreaded state called transaction id wraparound. This will mean a big chunk of downtime.

sentences.en: It’s worth monitoring this: if you use up all transaction ids in the system before they can be reclaimed by autovacuum, you’ll reach a dreaded state called transaction id wraparound.
sentences.zh: 值得监控的是：如果您在 autovacuum 回收之前用完系统中的所有事务 ID，您将达到称为事务 ID 环绕的可怕状态。

sentences.en: This will mean a big chunk of downtime.
sentences.zh: 这将意味着大量的停机时间。

blocks.html_en: Other types of bloat

sentences.en: Other types of bloat
sentences.zh: 其他类型的肿胀

blocks.html_en: Besides dead tuples, there are two other kinds of bloat you’ll often encounter in a busy Postgres system:

sentences.en: Besides dead tuples, there are two other kinds of bloat you’ll often encounter in a busy Postgres system:
sentences.zh: 除了死元组之外，在繁忙的 Postgres 系统中您还经常会遇到另外两种类型的膨胀：

blocks.html_en: • Table bloat caused by partially filled pages. Postgres stores rows on pages on disk, each of which are 8kb in size. When Postgres can’t fit new rows onto an existing page, it creates a new one. But when dead tuples are reclaimed, this can lead to pages not being entirely filled, which can increase the disk usage of Postgres, sometimes significantly. The best way to avoid table bloat is by tuning autovacuum before you’re bloated. But there are some extensions to help with bloated tables, like pg_repack, because the built-in Postgres VACUUM FULL is rarely a good idea. Note that Postgres 19 is getting REPACK...CONCURRENTLY, which I haven’t tested, but seems like potentially a good solution for concurrent table repacking.

sentences.en: Table bloat caused by partially filled pages.
sentences.zh: 部分填充的页面导致表膨胀。

sentences.en: Postgres stores rows on pages on disk, each of which are 8kb in size.
sentences.zh: Postgres 将行存储在磁盘上的页上，每个页的大小为 8kb。

sentences.en: When Postgres can’t fit new rows onto an existing page, it creates a new one.
sentences.zh: 当 Postgres 无法将新行放入现有页面时，它会创建一个新行。

sentences.en: But when dead tuples are reclaimed, this can lead to pages not being entirely filled, which can increase the disk usage of Postgres, sometimes significantly.
sentences.zh: 但是，当回收死元组时，这可能会导致页面未完全填满，这会增加 Postgres 的磁盘使用量，有时甚至会显着增加。

sentences.en: The best way to avoid table bloat is by tuning autovacuum before you’re bloated.
sentences.zh: 避免表膨胀的最佳方法是在膨胀之前调整 autovacuum。

sentences.en: But there are some extensions to help with bloated tables, like pg_repack, because the built-in Postgres VACUUM FULL is rarely a good idea.
sentences.zh: 但是有一些扩展可以帮助处理臃肿的表，例如 pg_repack，因为内置的 Postgres VACUUM FULL 很少是一个好主意。

sentences.en: Note that Postgres 19 is getting REPACK...CONCURRENTLY, which I haven’t tested, but seems like potentially a good solution for concurrent table repacking.
sentences.zh: 请注意，Postgres 19 正在 REPACK...CONCURRENTLY，我尚未对此进行测试，但似乎可能是并发表重新打包的一个很好的解决方案。

This isn’t good—long-running transactions prevent autovacuum from doing its job properly, and therefore make the entire system bloated with dead tuples. Also if you want to keep writing to the old tables, the new table won’t see the new data.

content_html_zh:
在过去的半年左右的时间里，我一直在为我们的工程师编写一份内部文档，试图将两年来的 Postgres 之战提炼成一份有一定凝聚力的文档。 虽然我很喜欢 Postgres 手册，但我发现当遇到麻烦时很难求助于它，因为它实在是太全面了。 我认为这可能对其他人有用，并且希望得到反馈（或您在生产中运行 Postgres 时学到的其他花絮）。

在开始 Hatchet 之前，虽然我对 SQL 很熟悉，但我的知识范围基本上是：如果查询很慢，就需要索引。 这是本文档的出发点；我假设您熟悉 SQL 基础知识、行、表，并且大致了解索引是什么。

如果克劳德写下你所有的查询，这可能是浪费时间！ 我推荐supabase/代理技能

关于 ORM 的快速说明

本指南应该仍然有用，但您可能需要将其中一些技巧转化为您选择的 ORM。 随着规模的扩大，ORM 无法进行大量优化，除非您能够突破抽象层并编写 SQL。 你可以优雅地或不优雅地这样做； Prisma TypedSQL 或同等产品对此看起来很有趣。 我们在 Hatchet 使用 sqlc，这使我们的行为非常相似；如果您是 Go 堆栈爱好者，强烈推荐。

目录

  • 简单的东西：良好的读取、写入和模式 编写良好的模式 编写良好的读取查询 编写高性能联接 复合索引并将 ORDER BY 与索引对齐 编写良好的写入查询 迁移 连接管理
  • 中级：查询规划器、批量更新和 autovacuum 引入最容易泄漏的抽象：查询规划器 有时，seq 扫描才有意义 写入大量数据 默认 autovacuum 设置可能会杀死数据库 其他类型的膨胀
  • 一些高级内容 FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED 大表迁移的分区技巧

简单的东西：良好的读取、写入和模式

让我们从基础知识开始：小批量的查询和模式。

编写一个好的架构

部署后，模式是迄今为止最难更改的，因此值得花一些时间在它们上。 我建议迭代地构建您的架构：从表和主键的粗略近似开始，然后根据您的应用程序需求在这些表上编写一些查询。 您可以用一些问题来近似这一点：这是一个高读取和/或高写入的表吗？ 最常见的读取过滤器是什么？ 我更新哪些栏目最多？

如果您想更正式地了解它，您可以研究数据库规范化为 1NF/2NF/3NF，但我发现规范形式有时与查询效率和易用性不一致，这在您快速移动时至关重要 - 有时将数据转储到 jsonb 列中更容易。

我的模式经验法则是：

  • 使用标识列（自动递增整数，比bigserial性能稍高）或内置UUID作为主键
  • 始终使用时间戳
  • 始终使用主键
  • 对于小容量表，使用带有级联删除的外键，特别是在数据库一致性和正确性很重要的情况下。 音量较大时要小心。

编写良好的读取查询

让我们从 SELECT 查询开始。 一个有用的（尽管有点不准确）快速选择的心理模型是：在幕后，Postgres 要么会非常快速地找到表中的一行，要么会使用称为顺序扫描的方法读取表中的每一行。

当您按以下条件过滤时，它会很快找到一行：

  • 显式索引
  • 唯一约束（只是索引的特例）
  • 主键（这些在 Postgres 中自动索引）

索引默认使用 btree 实现。 将索引视为 Postgres 中的另一个表是最有帮助的，其中数据以特定格式存储，该格式针对查找进行了优化（稍后会详细介绍）。 这些树很棒，因为找到一行大约需要 log(n) 时间，其中 n 是表中的行数，换句话说，速度非常快。

当 Postgres 无法使用索引时，它将使用称为顺序扫描或 seq 扫描的方法。 Seq 扫描比索引查找慢得多，但现代数据库将行加载到内存中的速度非常快，您可能一开始都没有注意到：对少于 20k 行的表进行 seq 扫描几乎是即时的。

编写表演者加入

对于内连接，很少有人争论不使用主键作为内连接；它通常涉及模式设计或规范化问题。 以与 WHERE 子句相同的方式对待 ON 子句 — 适用相同的原则。 使用索引。

复合索引并将 ORDER BY 与索引对齐

通常，应用程序中的第一个慢速查询将是跨大型表的列表查询。 像这样的东西：

正在加载语法高亮显示...

在这种情况下，您可以使用复合索引 - 明智的索引可能是：

正在加载语法高亮显示...

在更复杂的情况下，一个好的经验法则是：ORDER BY 列应该是索引中的最后一列，并且您应该将列与 ORDER BY 中的顺序对齐。 请注意，Postgres 可以双向扫描 btree，因此有时 DESC 是无关紧要的，但对于复合索引来说这是一个很好的做法。 更多信息请点击这里。

编写良好的写入查询

写入成功的前提是：

  1. 保持交易简短。 除非有充分的理由，否则不要在事务中间查询外部服务。
  2. 小心您锁定写入的行；换句话说，只锁定您需要的内容。 每次更新一行时，都会在短时间内对该行进行锁定，直到事务提交。

随着您的系统变得更加繁忙，您将开始更多地注意到锁的影响。 特别是，您可能会尝试在将来的某个时候使用简单的 CREATE INDEX 命令创建索引：结果这会锁定您的表并阻止插入和更新！ 在现有大型表上创建索引时，请始终使用 CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY。

迁移

真正擅长编写迁移是一项重要的技术优势：它可以帮助您更快地迭代并增加正常运行时间。 作为起点，尝试保持迁移附加（换句话说，不要删除或删除列）并尽可能在事务中运行它们；这将使回滚和部分迁移更容易处理。 当您变得更高级时，您可以开始研究扩展和收缩迁移。

良好迁移的最简单心理模型是：这是否会阻止我所有的写入？ 创建没有 CONCURRENTLY 的索引会阻止所有写入，因此您可能会遇到停机情况。 一般来说，调用 ALTER TABLE 的操作值得再看一下；例如，向非常大的表添加新的检查约束也会阻止您的写入（除非您使用 NOT VALID 关键字添加它）。

连接管理

每次对数据库执行事务或查询时，您都在利用连接。 连接在多个维度（CPU 和内存）都是昂贵的，高连接流失率会导致大量不必要的资源浪费，因此连接应该是长期存在的。 连接风暴（当您同时开始使用大量新连接时）也可能导致很难调试与内部 Postgres 锁相关的边缘情况。

由于所有这些连接枪，像 pgbouncer 这样的外部连接池非常棒！ 如果您出于某种原因无法添加它，那么内存连接池是第二个不错的选择。 例如，由于 Hatchet 是开源的，我们并不假设所有用户数据库都使用连接池，因此我们使用 pgxpool（Go 的内存连接池）来实现此目的。

中级：查询规划器、批量更新和 autovacuum

引入最泄漏的抽象，查询规划器

在某个时刻，您的查询可能会变得足够复杂，以至于简单的索引无法解决问题（并且您不应该无休止地向表中添加索引 - 它们会带来开销）。 查询可能涉及许多 JOIN 语句或不同类型的联接，其中查询数据的正确路径不清楚。

此时，您需要关注查询规划器。 查询规划器充其量只是一个有漏洞的抽象。 这是一个内部实现，你几乎无法控制它，但你必须知道它的自发行为，有时甚至是非理性的行为。 这就像与法学硕士一起工作！

查询规划器查看您传入的查询，并确定如何将您的查询转换为数据库中的一组内部操作。 例如，它可能会查看您的查询，并意识到它需要使用索引。 在理想的情况下，查询规划器会知道对于每个查询和参数集，要使用的完美计划。 但查询规划器基于有限的信息进行操作，有时它不会选择最佳选项。

这些有限的信息就是表统计信息。 其实你可以直接在Postgres中查询：

正在加载语法高亮显示...

这些统计数据是为每次分析收集的。 运行 autovacuum 时也会发生这种情况（见下文），因此更频繁的 autovacuum 也意味着您的查询统计信息将更加最新。 查询行为不正确的一个常见原因是分析不够频繁。

我认为将查询视为二进制（它们要么进行序列扫描，要么不进行序列扫描）很有用的原因是：对查询进行微优化越多，查询规划器出错的风险就越大。 如果您坚持通过主键和索引进行查询，查询规划器将会轻松得多。

假设您的查询中没有任何明显的错误，但它仍然很慢 - 您如何着手调试它？ 一些 Postgres 数据库提供商（例如 Google CloudSQL）会对您的查询进行采样并保存速度较慢的查询，但许多提供商不会这样做。 这就是 EXPLAIN ANALYZE 为您提供帮助的地方。 这将输出查询的查询计划并执行查询（在生产中小心运行此查询 - 您可以使用不带 ANALYZE 的 EXPLAIN 来获取查询计划），然后将其基于表统计信息的估计值与扫描的实际行数进行比较。 我通常将 sql 查询放在一个文件中，在其前面添加 EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, COSTS, VERBOSE, BUFFERS, FORMAT JSON) 前缀，然后运行：

正在加载语法高亮显示...

然后使用explain.dalibo.com可视化执行计划。

有时顺序扫描才有意义

在某些情况下，您认为应该使用索引，但查询计划程序仍然进行 seq 扫描，尽管表统计信息是最新的并且索引是有效的。 在这些情况下，Postgres 通常估计 seq 扫描的成本将小于索引扫描的成本。 索引扫描确实会带来一些开销；索引与表中的实际数据（称为堆）分开存储 - 查找堆中的所有行可能会很昂贵！

除非您可以显着地重构您的查询，否则您可能必须接受它将进行顺序扫描，或者考虑诸如分区之类的东西（更多内容见下文）。

写入大量数据

假设您的应用程序正在扩展，并且您需要快速写入大量数据。 每个查询都有一些与之相关的开销（与我们之前讨论的连接开销分开）：这包括到数据库的往返时间、内部应用程序连接池获取连接所需的时间以及 Postgres 处理查询所需的时间（包括一组内部 Postgres 锁，这些锁在高吞吐量场景中可能成为瓶颈）。

为了减少这种开销，我们可以将一批行打包到每个查询中。 最简单的方法是在隐式事务中一次性将所有查询发送到 Postgres 服务器（在 Go 中，我们可以使用 pgx 来执行 SendBatch）。 批处理非常强大：我们发现它可以将吞吐量提高约 10 倍。 我在这里写了更多相关内容以及一些快速写入数据的其他技巧。

默认 autovacuum 设置可能会杀死您的数据库

Autovacuum 是 Postgres 数据库中的一项关键操作，有时需要进行调整，尤其是在高写入场景下。 autovacuum 守护进程负责很多事情，包括清理死元组和管理事务 ID。

什么是死元组？ 元组是文件系统上行的实例。 每次更新或删除一行时，该行的一个版本都会保留在 Postgres 中，直到更新或删除该行之前启动的所有事务都已提交或回滚。 这些无法再被任何事务读取的行是死元组。

如果你写入数据的速度足够快，有时 autovacuum 无法跟上，这会让你很快进入一种非常不健康的状态。 当您在数据库上查询活动进程时，您会看到这一点：

正在加载语法高亮显示...

如果您看到 autovacuum 查询运行超过约 1 小时，您可能需要考虑更改 autovacuum 设置！ 请参阅这篇文章了解更多信息。

值得监控的是：如果您在 autovacuum 回收之前用完系统中的所有事务 ID，您将达到称为事务 ID 环绕的可怕状态。 这将意味着大量的停机时间。

其他类型的肿胀

除了死元组之外，在繁忙的 Postgres 系统中您还经常会遇到另外两种类型的膨胀：

  1. 部分填充的页面导致表膨胀。 Postgres 将行存储在磁盘上的页上，每个页的大小为 8kb。 当 Postgres 无法将新行放入现有页面时，它会创建一个新行。 但是，当回收死元组时，这可能会导致页面未完全填满，这会增加 Postgres 的磁盘使用量，有时甚至会显着增加。 避免表膨胀的最佳方法是在膨胀之前调整 autovacuum。 但是有一些扩展可以帮助处理臃肿的表，例如 pg_repack，因为内置的 Postgres VACUUM FULL 很少是一个好主意。 请注意，Postgres 19 正在 REPACK...CONCURRENTLY，我尚未对此进行测试，但似乎可能是并发表重新打包的一个很好的解决方案。
  2. 索引膨胀是表膨胀的一种特殊情况，同样可以通过良好的 autovacuum 设置来解决。 但是 Postgres 有一个内置命令来处理这个问题，那就是 REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY。

分区

Postgres 具有内置分区，允许您根据行值（例如时间戳或哈希值）细分表。 这对于时间序列数据（在我们的例子中是历史任务数据）非常有用，因为：

  1. 每个分区都可以独立自动吸尘，允许您在桌子上扩展自动吸尘器
  2. 删除旧数据几乎是即时的 - 您只需删除表分区，而不是迭代行

分区确实有缺点：如果 Postgres 在规划阶段不修剪分区，则读取查询可能会产生开销（Postgres 在最近的版本中在这方面做得更好）。

大表迁移的技巧

（请注意，这并不是指数据库迁移，而是指将大量数据从一个表移动到另一个表，我们发现自己每年都会这样做几次）

如果您尝试迁移非常大的表，则在单个事务中复制数据可能需要几个小时。 这不好——长时间运行的事务会阻止 autovacuum 正常工作，从而使整个系统因死元组而变得臃肿。 此外，如果您想继续写入旧表，新表将看不到新数据。

因此，我们需要弄清楚如何在没有事务的情况下安全地迁移数据，并将新的写入（迁移开始后）复制到新表。 我们学到的技巧之一是使用 Postgres 触发器并在事务之外运行大批量回填，对主键使用唯一约束来防止重复写入。
